sections.title: Overview
sections.content: Gastro-oesophageal reflux causes retrograde movement of gastric contents into the oesophagus and may present as heartburn, regurgitation, or upper abdominal discomfort. In the prehospital setting, the main priority is to distinguish uncomplicated reflux from acute coronary syndrome, upper GI bleeding, perforation, or obstructive pathology.

sections.title: Clinical Presentation
sections.content:
- Burning retrosternal or epigastric discomfort
- Acid taste, regurgitation, belching, or post-prandial worsening
- Cough, hoarseness, or nocturnal symptoms in some patients
- Red-flag features may include dysphagia, odynophagia, GI bleeding, persistent vomiting, weight loss, or severe chest pain

sections.title: Considerations
sections.content:
- Chest pain should be treated as potentially cardiac until a safer explanation is established
- Red-flag symptoms warrant medical review rather than repeated symptomatic treatment
- NSAIDs, alcohol, obesity, pregnancy, and some medicines may worsen reflux symptoms

sections.title: Management – Primary
sections.content:
- Primary Survey and focused history
- Consider simple antacid therapy within scope for a classic low-risk reflux presentation
- Encourage upright posture and avoidance of immediate recumbency after meals
- Provide safety-net advice if pain changes, persists, or develops cardiac or GI red flags

sections.title: Management – Advanced
sections.content:
- Obtain an ECG when chest pain is prominent, atypical, exertional, or associated with cardiac risk factors
- Doctor-directed acid suppression may be considered in selected recurrent presentations
- Escalate for suspected ACS, upper GI bleeding, perforation, or inability to swallow

sections.title: Additional Information
sections.content:
- Lifestyle advice may include avoiding late meals, known dietary triggers, smoking, and alcohol where relevant
- Do not let temporary response to antacid therapy falsely reassure you if concerning features are present
